No. In FY2022, 3M had net sales of $34,229 million and purchases of property, plant and equipment of $1,749 million, which is a relatively small share of sales, while net cash provided by operating activities was $5,591 million.

As of FY2022, 3M’s operating income margin decreased year-on-year excluding special items to 20.8% from 22.2%, driven primarily by a 2.4 percentage-point raw material impact from higher raw material and logistics costs, partly offset by a 1.0 percentage-point benefit from organic growth/productivity and other factors such as strong pricing, spending discipline, and restructuring actions. Including special items, margin fell to 19.1% from 20.8%, as net costs for significant litigation, divestiture costs, Russia exit charges, and PFAS manufacturing exit costs more than offset the gain on business divestitures.

Safety and Industrial. Excluding M&A impacts, its organic growth was negatively impacted by a 4.5 percentage point decline from disposable respirator sales within personal safety, dragging down 3M’s overall growth in 2022.

Based on the June 30, 2023 balance sheet, 3M’s quick ratio was approximately 0.85x, calculated as cash and cash equivalents ($4,258 million) plus current marketable securities ($56 million) plus accounts receivable ($4,947 million), divided by total current liabilities ($10,936 million). Based on this quick ratio alone, 3M would not appear to have a particularly strong liquidity profile because quick assets were less than current liabilities. However, the document states that 3M “maintains a strong liquidity profile” and that its primary short-term liquidity needs are met through cash on hand and U.S. commercial paper issuances, so the quick ratio alone is not sufficient to assess its liquidity.
None are listed in the provided Q2 2023 Form 10-Q content. The document sections provided do not identify any debt securities registered to trade on a national securities exchange under 3M's name.

Yes. 3M states it expects to continue returning cash to shareholders through dividends, and the dividends declared were relatively consistent at $848 million in the second quarter of 2022, $828 million in the second quarter of 2023, $1,700 million in the first six months of 2022, and $1,655 million in the first six months of 2023.

I’m sorry, but the provided document content does not include the numerical values from Adobe’s FY2017 consolidated balance sheet or cash flow statement needed to calculate the operating cash flow ratio.

Yes. Adobe’s operating margin improved from approximately 32.9% in fiscal 2020 (operating income of $4.237 billion on revenue of $12.868 billion) to 36.8% in fiscal 2021 ($5.802 billion on $15.785 billion), then was essentially stable to slightly lower at 34.6% in fiscal 2022 ($6.098 billion on $17.606 billion), so as of FY2022 the profile shows improvement versus FY2020 but not continued improvement versus FY2021.

Yes. Cash flows from operations increased to $7.84 billion in fiscal 2022 from $7.23 billion in fiscal 2021, while investing cash outflows decreased to $570 million in fiscal 2022 from $3.54 billion in fiscal 2021, indicating improving free cash flow conversion as of FY2022.

Conventional inventory turnover is not a meaningful metric for AES because it is an electricity generation and distribution company, and its inventory “primarily consists of fuel and other raw materials used to generate power, and operational spare parts and supplies used to maintain power generation and distribution facilities,” rather than merchandise held for sale. Using FY2022 total revenue of $12,617 million as a rough proxy and average inventory of (($1,055 million + $604 million) / 2) = $829.5 million, the rough inventory turnover ratio is about 15.2x.
Amcor’s FY2020 year-end net accounts receivable, based on the balance sheet line item “Trade receivables, net,” was $1,615.9 million.

The key agenda of Amcor’s July 1, 2022 Form 8-K was the execution of supplemental indentures relating to the substitution of Amcor Flexibles North America, Inc. for Amcor Finance (USA), Inc. as issuer and the assumption by the substitute issuer of the former issuer’s covenants under the indentures governing the 2026 and 2028 notes.

AMCOR's quick ratio improved slightly from approximately 0.53 in FY2022 to approximately 0.57 in FY2023, calculated as (cash and cash equivalents plus trade receivables) divided by total current liabilities. This is a financial metric that an analyst could ask about because it measures short-term liquidity.

In FY2023, Amcor completed three acquisitions: a Czech Republic flexible packaging manufacturing plant on August 1, 2022 for $59 million, a medical device packaging manufacturing site in Shanghai, China on March 17, 2023 for $60 million, and a New Zealand-based manufacturer of automated protein packaging machines on May 31, 2023 for $45 million. In FY2022, the document does not report any acquisitions, only the disposal of non-core assets in the Flexibles reporting segment. In FY2021, the document does not report any acquisitions, only disposals of a non-core European hospital supplies business, two non-core businesses in India and Argentina, and the sale of its equity investment in AMVIG Holdings Limited.

AMCOR primarily operates in the packaging industry, as it is a global leader in developing and producing responsible packaging for food, beverage, pharmaceutical, medical, home and personal-care, and other products.

No. Amcor’s gross profit as a percentage of net sales decreased to 18.5% in fiscal year 2023 from 19.4% in fiscal year 2022, mainly due to the impact of the pass-through of higher raw material costs and disposed operations, so gross margin is not showing an improving profile as of FY2023.

As of December 31, 2022, Amcor’s restructuring liability was $93 million, consisting of employee costs of $81 million, fixed asset related costs of $2 million, and other costs of $10 million. It related primarily to restructuring activities associated with the Russia-Ukraine conflict, and the company expects the majority of these employee, fixed asset related, and other costs to be paid within the next twelve months.
$2,018 million.
FY2015 depreciation and amortization was $167 million and net revenue was $3,991 million, implying a D&A margin of 4.2% ($167 ÷ $3,991).

Yes. Based on FY22 balance sheet amounts, AMD’s quick ratio was about 1.57 (($4,835 cash and cash equivalents + $1,020 short-term investments + $4,126 accounts receivable + $2 receivables from related parties) / $6,369 current liabilities), which indicates a reasonably healthy liquidity profile.

AMD primarily sells: server CPUs, GPUs, DPUs, FPGAs and Adaptive SoC products for data centers; CPUs, APUs and chipsets for desktop and notebook PCs; discrete GPUs; semi-custom SoC products and development services; and embedded CPUs, GPUs, APUs, FPGAs and Adaptive SoC products. It also offers design platforms and services, including adaptable platforms and software development platforms for FPGAs and Adaptive SoCs, and from time to time may sell or license portions of its intellectual property portfolio.

AMD’s 2022 revenue increase to $23.6 billion from $16.4 billion in 2021 was driven by higher Data Center sales from EPYC server processors, higher Gaming revenue from semi-custom product sales due to stronger demand for gaming console SoCs, and a significant increase in Embedded revenue from the inclusion of Xilinx embedded product revenue following the February 2022 acquisition of Xilinx. These increases were partially offset by a 10% decline in Client revenue caused by a 24% decrease in unit shipments due to challenging PC market conditions and significant inventory correction across the PC supply chain in the second half of 2022.

Operating margin is a useful metric for AMD, and it declined in FY22: operating income fell to $1.3 billion from $3.6 billion in 2021 while gross margin fell to 45% from 48%. The change was primarily driven by amortization of intangible assets associated with the Xilinx acquisition, along with sharply higher operating expenses including higher research and development spending, higher marketing, general and administrative expenses, and acquisition-related costs; segment results were also affected by weaker Client revenue due to a weak PC market and inventory correction actions.
In FY22, operating activities brought in the most cash flow, with net cash provided of $3.6 billion. Investing activities provided $2.0 billion, while financing activities used $3.3 billion.
Data Center. Excluding Embedded, Data Center sales increased the most proportionally, rising from $3,694 million in 2021 to $6,043 million in 2022.
Yes. AMD reported customer concentration in FY22, stating that one customer accounted for 16% of consolidated net revenue for the year ended December 31, 2022.
American Express Company’s Fixed Rate Senior Notes, Floating Rate Senior Notes, Fixed-to-Floating Rate Senior Notes, Fixed Rate Subordinated Notes and Fixed-to-Floating Rate Subordinated Notes.

American Express primarily operates in the United States, EMEA, APAC, and LACC as of 2022. EMEA represents Europe, the Middle East and Africa; APAC represents Asia Pacific, Australia and New Zealand; and LACC represents Latin America, Canada and the Caribbean.

Operating margin is not a particularly useful metric for American Express because, as a financial company, its performance is presented using “total revenues net of interest expense,” provisions for credit losses, and expenses, with credit costs materially affecting profitability. Based on the statements of income, pretax income as a share of total revenues net of interest expense declined to about 18.1% in 2022 ($9,585 / $52,862) from about 25.2% in 2021 ($10,689 / $42,380), so there was not an improving margin profile in 2022.

Gross margin is not a useful metric for American Express because it is a payments and lending company, and its results are presented through “total revenues net of interest expense,” provisions for credit losses and operating expenses rather than cost of goods sold and gross profit. For FY2022, performance was driven by higher discount revenue from a 23 percent increase in billed business, higher net card fees from growth in premium card portfolios, higher service fees and other revenue from foreign exchange-related revenues, travel commissions and delinquency fees, higher processed revenue from increased volumes, and higher interest income from higher average Card Member loan balances and interest rates, partly offset by higher interest expense from higher rates paid on deposits and debt outstanding.
The effective tax rate decreased by 3.0 percentage points, from 24.6% in 2021 to 21.6% in 2022.
Customer deposits were the largest liability on American Express’s 2022 Balance Sheet at $110.239 billion.

Yes. American Express increased cards-in-force to 133.3 million in 2022 from 121.7 million in 2021, and proprietary cards-in-force rose to 76.7 million from 71.4 million, indicating it retained and grew its Card Member base during 2022.

No. Based on FY2022 data, current assets were $1,250 million and current liabilities were $2,811 million, so working capital was negative $1,561 million.  
Working capital may be of limited usefulness for American Water Works because it is a capital-intensive, rate-regulated business that funds a majority of its capital with cash flows from operations and external financing, and it states that short-term requirements are supported by access to debt and equity capital markets, a revolving credit facility, and cash flows from operations.

Yes. Best Buy’s gross profit as a percentage of revenue was 21.4% in fiscal 2023, 22.5% in fiscal 2022, and 22.4% in fiscal 2021, so the year-to-year changes were about 1.1 percentage points and 0.1 percentage points, which is within roughly 2% each year.

In FY2023, no major acquisitions are listed. In FY2022, Best Buy acquired Current Health Ltd. on November 2, 2021 for net cash consideration of $389 million and Two Peaks, LLC d/b/a Yardbird Furniture on November 4, 2021 for net cash consideration of $79 million. In FY2021, no major acquisitions are listed.
Operating activities brought in the most cash flow for Best Buy in FY2023, providing $1,824 million. Investing activities used $962 million, and financing activities used $1,806 million.
Yes. Cash and cash equivalents decreased from $1,874 million at January 28, 2023 (FY 2023) to $1,093 million at July 29, 2023 (Q2 FY2024), a drop of $781 million.
Yes. Best Buy stores at the end of Q2 decreased to 907 in FY2024 from 930 in FY2023, a decline of 23 stores.
Computing and Mobile Phones performed the best by top line in the domestic market during Q2 FY2024, with 41% of revenue mix.

Yes. In FY2022, Defense, Space & Security represented about 35% of total revenues ($23,162 million of $66,608 million), Global Services represented about 26% ($17,241 million of $66,608 million), and revenues from the U.S. government represented 40% of consolidated revenues.

Yes. In FY2022, Boeing disclosed materially important ongoing legal matters including remaining pending lawsuits and other matters related to the 737 MAX accidents, for which it could not reasonably estimate a range of loss not covered by insurance, and an ongoing arbitration with Embraer over terminated joint venture agreements, for which it also could not reasonably estimate a range of loss.

Gross margin is not a particularly useful metric for Boeing as of FY2022 because its cost recognition is heavily affected by program accounting and long-term contract accounting, which rely on estimated program and contract margins and can produce large cumulative catch-up adjustments and immediate reach-forward losses. While cost of sales as a percentage of revenues improved slightly to 94.7% in 2022 from 95.2% in 2021, the company states this remained “largely consistent,” and its accounting policies mean gross margin can be materially distorted by estimate changes rather than underlying period economics.

Boeing’s primary customers in FY2022 were the commercial airline industry worldwide for its Commercial Airplanes segment, and commercial and defense customers worldwide for its Global Services segment. Its Defense, Space & Security segment also serves government and commercial customers through military, intelligence, satellite, and space products and services.

Yes. Boeing states that the airline industry has historically been cyclical and very competitive, and because it depends heavily on commercial airlines, market conditions significantly impact demand for its commercial aircraft and related services.

Boeing forecasts further gradual increases in 737 production from the 31 per month rate reached in 2022, based on market demand and supply chain capacity. For the 787, it expects to gradually return production to 5 per month in 2023.
Boeing’s effective tax rate was (0.6)% in FY2022 versus 14.7% in FY2021. This represents a decrease of 15.3 percentage points.
FY2019-FY2021 average unadjusted operating income % margin was 11.3%.
Corning’s effective tax rate increased from 20.2% in FY2021 to 22.9% in FY2022, a change of 2.7 percentage points.

Yes. Corning had positive working capital in FY2022: $2,278 million, with a current ratio of 1.4:1.  
The document also states that Corning funds its working capital with cash from operations and short-term borrowings, including commercial paper when necessary, and also receives upfront cash from customers under long-term supply agreements and cash incentives from government entities.

No. In FY2022, CVS Health had approximately $2.7 billion of gross capital expenditures and $13.1 billion of net property and equipment versus total assets of $228.3 billion, indicating relatively modest capital intensity.

Yes. CVS Health said it was involved in numerous ongoing legal and regulatory proceedings, but also stated that none of its accruals for outstanding legal matters were material individually or in the aggregate to its financial condition and that it did not believe any pending matters would have a material adverse effect on its financial position.
Yes. CVS Health Corporation has paid cash dividends every quarter since becoming a public company, and the quarterly cash dividend declared by the Board was $0.55 per share in 2022.
Yes. Mary N. Dillon previously served as Chief Executive Officer of Ulta Beauty, Inc. from July 2013 through June 2021.

No. In FY2022, Johnson & Johnson’s worldwide sales increased only 1.3% to $94.9 billion, versus 13.6% in 2021, and its five-year and ten-year compound annual growth rates for worldwide sales were 4.4% and 3.5%, respectively. Net earnings also declined to $17.9 billion in 2022 from $20.9 billion in 2021.

Gross margin is a useful metric for J&J, and it declined slightly in FY2022: gross profit was $63.854 billion on sales of $94.943 billion versus $63.920 billion on sales of $93.775 billion in FY2021, implying gross margin fell from about 68.2% to 67.3%. The change was driven by cost of products sold increasing as a percent of sales due to one-time COVID-19 vaccine manufacturing exit related costs, currency impacts in the Pharmaceutical segment, and commodity inflation in the MedTech and Consumer Health segments, partially offset by supply chain benefits in the Consumer Health segment.

Approximately 2.7 times, calculated as FY2022 cost of products sold of $31,089 million divided by average inventory of $11,435 million [($12,483 million + $10,387 million) / 2]. Inventory turnover is meaningful for Johnson & Johnson because it reports inventories and cost of products sold.

Yes. Full-year 2022 adjusted EPS grew 3.6% to $10.15 from $9.80, and FY2023 guidance indicates adjusted operational growth of 3.0%–5.0% with a 4.0% midpoint, implying slightly faster growth at the midpoint.
In FY2022, J&J’s U.S. sales grew 3.0%, while international sales declined 0.6% on a reported basis. On an operational basis, international sales grew 9.1% versus 3.0% for U.S. sales.
JnJ’s Consumer Health business will be treated as a discontinued operation from August 30, 2023 onward.

Gross margin is not a relevant metric for JPMorgan Chase because it is a bank, and its results are presented in terms of net interest income, noninterest revenue, and noninterest expense rather than sales less cost of goods sold. The document reports total net revenue, noninterest revenue, and net interest income for 2022, 2021, and 2020, but does not present gross margin.
